top_vector_tech_docs.content:
# FUEL 

Fuel storage system

In flight, NACA scoops located in each surge tank provide ram air pressure to maintain a positive pressure on the fuel in the tanks. During ground operations, NACA scoops provide static ventilation of the tanks and relieve the buildup of air pressure during refueling or thermal expansion of the fuel.
![The image depicts a schematic diagram of a fluid management system from an A220 technical document. The system includes several key components L SURGE TANK Located on the left side, this tank is connected to a surge tank with a valve and a pressure gauge. L MAIN TANK Positioned adjacent to the L Surge Tank, this tank is connected to the L Collector Tank via a pipe with a valve. L COLLECTOR TANK Situated between the L Main Tank and the Center Tank, this tank has a valve and a pressure gauge. L DRY BAY Above the L Collector Tank, this area includes a valve and a pressure gauge. CENTER TANK Positioned centrally, this tank connects the left and right sides of the system. R COLLECTOR TANK Located on the right side, this tank is connected to the Center Tank with a valve and a pressure gauge. R MAIN TANK Positioned adjacent to the R Collector Tank, this tank has a valve and a pressure gauge. R SURGE TANK Located on the right side, this tank is connected to a surge tank with a valve and a pressure gauge. R DRY BAY Above the R Collector Tank, this area includes a valve and a pressure gauge. The diagram also shows various pipes and valves connecting these components, facilitating the flow and management of fluids within the system.](img-0.jpeg)

top_rrf_tech_docs.content:
## DETAILED COMPONENT INFORMATION

## RESERVOIR

Hydraulic systems no. 1 and no. 2 reservoirs are divided into high-pressure and low-pressure chambers. A seal drain at the low-pressure chamber routes piston seal leakage to an ecology bottle. The reservoir manual depressurization valve limits pressure within the reservoir high-pressure chamber. When manually actuated, it dumps system high-pressure into the reservoir low-pressure chamber.
The reservoir bleed/relief valve feature limits pressure within the low-pressure chamber. The relief valve has a cracking pressure of 145 psi , and resets at 90 psi . This valve, when manually actuated, bleeds fluid pressure from the low-pressure side of the reservoir and into the reservoir ecology bottle.
When low-pressure chamber piston (A1) contacts the suction shutoff valve seal area, it closes hydraulic fluid supply flow to the hydraulic pumps. If a case drain line breaks in the fuel tank, closing the suction shutoff valve prevents the hydraulic pump from running dry. Fuel ingest into the hydraulic system is a fire hazard.
A bootstrap reservoir uses a dual-piston where high-pressure hydraulic from the hydraulic pump outlet is applied to the small area of the piston (A2). This produces a low-pressure on the reservoir side of the piston. A major advantage of bootstrap reservoirs is that reservoir pressurization is maintained during aggressive flight maneuvers, including negative-G flight.

The high-pressure line to the reservoir includes a check valve. The check valve maintains reservoir pressure after the pump has been shut down, and hydraulic pump inlet pressure when the engine-driven pump (EDP) is not rotating.

The high-pressure chamber piston (A2) drives the low-pressure piston (A1) to contact the seal area and close the suction shutoff valve. The closed valve deprives the pump(s) of fluid, causing them to vapor lock and stop pumping.
The reservoir is equipped with a return port and a high-pressure port. A fluid quantity transducer provides electronic fluid quantity indication on the hydraulic synoptic page in the flight deck. The reservoir also includes visual quantity indication for maintenance personnel. The visual indicator provides an indication of reservoir fluid level through a cable-driven slider linked to a piston position. A temperature transducer, mounted to the reservoir module, senses the temperature of the fluid in the low-pressure chamber.

A reservoir return check valve prevents fluid backflow to the return filter module when the hydraulic system is not operating.
A temperature transducer monitors the reservoir. If the temperature reaches $107^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(224.6^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ an the engine indication and crew alerting system (EICAS) HYD 1 (2) HI TEMP caution message is displayed. When the hydraulic fluid temperature exceeds $125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(257^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$, the temperature switch closes the hydraulic firewall shutoff valve (FWSOV). This prevents hydraulic fluid flow to the inlet of the engine-driven pump (EDP), disabling its operation without requiring the engine to be shut down.

A eutectic fuse, also referred to as a thermal fuse, is mounted on each system reservoir in the low-pressure chamber. The fuse is designed to melt at $144^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(291.2^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$, and drains hydraulic fluid overboard.
A maintenance-free accumulator in hydraulic systems no. 1 and no. 2 is connected to its respective reservoir high-pressure chamber, and continues to pressurize the system after the hydraulic pumps have been shut down. The accumulator is precharged with helium and is maintenance free.

top_rrf_tech_docs.content:
# A. Motive flow 

Motive flow is the primary means to move fuel throughout the fuel distribution system. When the high pressure fuel from the Engine Driven Pump (EDP) passes through a venturi-shaped fuel ejector, a low pressure is created (refer to Figure 11-03-1). The suction caused by the low pressure moves the fuel out of the tank.

The fuel distribution system uses motive flow to:

- Transfer fuel from the center tank into the main tanks,
- Transfer fuel from the main tanks to the collector tanks, and
- Supply fuel to the engines and APU.
![The image depicts a fuel supply system diagram for an A220 aircraft. Highpressure fuel from an enginedriven pump is directed into the system. The fuel flows through a series of passages indicated by arrows moving from left to right. A venturi is shown in the lower part of the diagram, where suction is created. This suction aids in the fuel supply process, leading to the resulting fuel supply at the end of the system.](img-0.jpeg)

Motive flow ejector pump
Figure 11-03-1

## B. AC boost pumps

An AC boost pump in each collector tank is the backup for the main fuel ejector pump. The AC boost pumps are AC electrical pumps that provide the functions that follow:

- Engine fuel feed backup,
- APU fuel feed backup,
- Fuel crossfeed,
- Fuel transfer, and

APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) The APU is connected to the fuel system for power supply. The diagram shows the flow of fuel from the main tanks and surge tanks to the engines, with the collector tanks facilitating the distribution. The green lines indicate the primary fuel flow paths, while the black lines represent other connections within the system.](img-0.jpeg)

# A. Motive flow 

Motive flow is the primary means to move fuel throughout the fuel distribution system. When the high pressure fuel from the Engine Driven Pump (EDP) passes through a venturi-shaped fuel ejector, a low pressure is created (refer to Figure 11-03-1). The suction caused by the low pressure moves the fuel out of the tank.

The fuel distribution system uses motive flow to:

- Transfer fuel from the center tank into the main tanks,
- Transfer fuel from the main tanks to the collector tanks, and
- Supply fuel to the engines and APU.
![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

Motive flow ejector pump
Figure 11-03-1

## B. AC boost pumps

An AC boost pump in each collector tank is the backup for the main fuel ejector pump. The AC boost pumps are AC electrical pumps that provide the functions that follow:

- Engine fuel feed backup,
- APU fuel feed backup,
- Fuel crossfeed,
- Fuel transfer, and

1.7 On the pressure refuel adapter, set the REFUEL /DEFUEL selector to DEFUEL.
1.8 Set the REFUEL/DEFUEL switch to the DEFUEL position.
1.9 Set the AUTO/MANUAL switch to the MANUAL position.
1.10 Attach the hose to the refuel adapter. Start the fuel truck pump, which must be configured in the suction mode.
1.11 Fuel is suctioned out of the wing tanks.
1.12 Set the POWER SWITCH to the ON position. The PRESEL display window shows DEFUEL.
1.13 Monitor the LEFT/CENTER/RIGHT display window and stop the fuel truck pump when desired quantity is reached.
1.14 Manually close center tank MANUAL DEFUEL valve when the desired quantity in the center wing tank is reached.
1.15 Defuel the aircraft to a minimum quantity or to the quantity that you calculated.

# Note 

Defueling will stop when the fuel level in one of the main fuel tanks goes below the inlet suction devise. Defueling will also stop if the fuel level in the center tank goes below the inlet. A large quantity of fuel can remain in the center tank.
1.16 Set the MANUAL LEFT/RIGHT switch(es) to the CLOSE position to stop defueling in all tanks.

top_rewritten_tech_docs.content:
## DETAILED COMPONENT INFORMATION

## RESERVOIR

Hydraulic systems no. 1 and no. 2 reservoirs are divided into high-pressure and low-pressure chambers. A seal drain at the low-pressure chamber routes piston seal leakage to an ecology bottle. The reservoir manual depressurization valve limits pressure within the reservoir high-pressure chamber. When manually actuated, it dumps system high-pressure into the reservoir low-pressure chamber.
The reservoir bleed/relief valve feature limits pressure within the low-pressure chamber. The relief valve has a cracking pressure of 145 psi , and resets at 90 psi . This valve, when manually actuated, bleeds fluid pressure from the low-pressure side of the reservoir and into the reservoir ecology bottle.
When low-pressure chamber piston (A1) contacts the suction shutoff valve seal area, it closes hydraulic fluid supply flow to the hydraulic pumps. If a case drain line breaks in the fuel tank, closing the suction shutoff valve prevents the hydraulic pump from running dry. Fuel ingest into the hydraulic system is a fire hazard.
A bootstrap reservoir uses a dual-piston where high-pressure hydraulic from the hydraulic pump outlet is applied to the small area of the piston (A2). This produces a low-pressure on the reservoir side of the piston. A major advantage of bootstrap reservoirs is that reservoir pressurization is maintained during aggressive flight maneuvers, including negative-G flight.

The high-pressure line to the reservoir includes a check valve. The check valve maintains reservoir pressure after the pump has been shut down, and hydraulic pump inlet pressure when the engine-driven pump (EDP) is not rotating.

The high-pressure chamber piston (A2) drives the low-pressure piston (A1) to contact the seal area and close the suction shutoff valve. The closed valve deprives the pump(s) of fluid, causing them to vapor lock and stop pumping.
The reservoir is equipped with a return port and a high-pressure port. A fluid quantity transducer provides electronic fluid quantity indication on the hydraulic synoptic page in the flight deck. The reservoir also includes visual quantity indication for maintenance personnel. The visual indicator provides an indication of reservoir fluid level through a cable-driven slider linked to a piston position. A temperature transducer, mounted to the reservoir module, senses the temperature of the fluid in the low-pressure chamber.

A reservoir return check valve prevents fluid backflow to the return filter module when the hydraulic system is not operating.
A temperature transducer monitors the reservoir. If the temperature reaches $107^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(224.6^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ an the engine indication and crew alerting system (EICAS) HYD 1 (2) HI TEMP caution message is displayed. When the hydraulic fluid temperature exceeds $125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(257^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$, the temperature switch closes the hydraulic firewall shutoff valve (FWSOV). This prevents hydraulic fluid flow to the inlet of the engine-driven pump (EDP), disabling its operation without requiring the engine to be shut down.

A eutectic fuse, also referred to as a thermal fuse, is mounted on each system reservoir in the low-pressure chamber. The fuse is designed to melt at $144^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(291.2^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$, and drains hydraulic fluid overboard.
A maintenance-free accumulator in hydraulic systems no. 1 and no. 2 is connected to its respective reservoir high-pressure chamber, and continues to pressurize the system after the hydraulic pumps have been shut down. The accumulator is precharged with helium and is maintenance free.

# FUEL 

Fuel storage system

In flight, NACA scoops located in each surge tank provide ram air pressure to maintain a positive pressure on the fuel in the tanks. During ground operations, NACA scoops provide static ventilation of the tanks and relieve the buildup of air pressure during refueling or thermal expansion of the fuel.

L Collector Tank Positioned below the L Dry Bay, it is connected to the center tank via a fluid line. Center Tank Located centrally, it serves as a central fluid reservoir. R Collector Tank Positioned below the center tank, it is connected to the R Dry Bay. R Dry Bay Situated above the R Collector Tank, it includes a dry bay valve. R MAIN TANK Positioned adjacent to the R Dry Bay, it is connected to the R Surge Tank. R SURGE TANK Located on the right side, this tank is connected to a surge tank valve. The diagram also includes various valves and fluid lines that connect these components, facilitating the flow of fluid between the tanks and dry bays.](img-0.jpeg)

top_rewritten_tech_docs.content:
In an aircraft recovery situation, it may be necessary to defuel the aircraft. The different defuel scenarios are listed in this data module.

# 2 Fuel storage 

The aircraft has three fuel tanks. Two tanks are installed in the left and right wings and an integral center tank installed on the fuselage structure.

The left and the right wing tanks have a main tank, a collector tank, and a surge tank. The main and the collector tanks are used to store the fuel. The surge tank, located at the wing tip, collects the fuel that can enter the vent system during aircraft maneuvers.

The collector tanks are partially sealed and are located inboard of each wing tank. The collector tanks contain the fuel to feed the engines.

The fuel vent system maintains air pressure inside the fuel tanks within the permitted limits by providing air flow path between the fuel tanks and the atmosphere in all operating conditions. Each fuel tank is connected to a wing surge tank through a vent pipe. Each fuel tank has the pressure relief valves to prevent too much pressure in the tank.

For more information about the fuel tank locations, refer to Fig. 1 .
For more information about aircraft fuel loads, refer to the Weight and Balance Manual.

top_rewritten_tech_docs.content:
```markdown
# Aircraft Fuel System Diagram

This diagram illustrates the fuel system of an Airbus A220 aircraft, highlighting the main components and their connections.

## Components

- **L ENG**: Left engine
- **R ENG**: Right engine
- **CENTER TANK**: Central fuel storage tank
- **L MAIN TANK**: Left main fuel tank
- **R MAIN TANK**: Right main fuel tank
- **L SURGE TANK**: Left surge tank
- **R SURGE TANK**: Right surge tank
- **COLLECTOR TANK**: Tank that collects fuel from various sources
- **APU**: Auxiliary Power Unit

## Connections

- Fuel flows from the **L ENG** and **R ENG** to the **CENTER TANK**.
- The **L MAIN TANK** and **R MAIN TANK** are connected to the **CENTER TANK**.
- **COLLECTOR TANKS** are positioned between the **MAIN TANKS** and the **CENTER TANK**.
- **SURGE TANKS** are connected to the **MAIN TANKS** and **COLLECTOR TANKS**.
- The **APU** is connected to the fuel system for auxiliary power supply.

## Flow Path

- Fuel is drawn from the **L SURGE TANK** and **R SURGE TANK** to the respective engines (**L ENG** and **R ENG**).
- The **COLLECTOR TANKS** facilitate the transfer of fuel between the **MAIN TANKS** and the **CENTER TANK**.
- The **APU** can draw fuel from the system as needed.
```

![The image depicts the fuel tank configuration of an A220 aircraft. It includes the following components Left Surge Tank Left Main Tank Center Tank Right Main Tank Right Surge Tank Each of the main tanks contains a Collector Tank and a Dry Bay. The layout shows the symmetrical arrangement of the fuel tanks on either side of the center tank, with surge tanks positioned at the outermost ends.](img-0.jpeg)

Fuel storage system
Figure 11-02-1

# B. Fuel tank ventilation 

The fuel vent system (refer to Figure 11-02-2) maintains the air pressure inside the fuel tanks within the structural design requirements by providing an airflow path between the fuel tanks and the atmosphere in all operating conditions. Each fuel tank is vented to a wing surge tank through a dedicated tank vent system.

The center tank and left main tank are vented into the left surge tank. The right main tank is vented into the right surge tank. The float vent valves close the vent lines when the tanks are near full or when the aircraft is in an attitude that would cause fuel to enter the vent tank lines. Each main tank has a pressure relief valve. The center tank has two alternate vent tank lines, both with a pressure relief valve attached.

```markdown
# Aircraft Fuel System Diagram

This diagram illustrates the fuel system of an Airbus A220 aircraft, highlighting the key components and their interconnections.

## Components

- **L ENG**: Left engine
- **R ENG**: Right engine
- **L MAIN TANK**: Left main fuel tank
- **R MAIN TANK**: Right main fuel tank
- **L SURGE TANK**: Left surge tank
- **R SURGE TANK**: Right surge tank
- **CENTER TANK**: Central fuel tank
- **COLLECTOR TANK**: Fuel collector tank
- **DRY BAY**: Dry bay area
- **AC BOOST PUMPS**: Air conditioning boost pumps
- **APU**: Auxiliary Power Unit

## Connections

- Fuel flows from the **L MAIN TANK** and **R MAIN TANK** to their respective engines (**L ENG** and **R ENG**) through the dry bay.
- The **CENTER TANK** is connected to both the left and right main tanks via AC boost pumps.
- **COLLECTOR TANKS** are positioned between the main tanks and the surge tanks, facilitating fuel transfer.
- The **APU** draws fuel from the surge tanks.

This layout ensures efficient fuel distribution and management across the aircraft's fuel system.
```

top_rewritten_tech_docs.content:
```markdown
# Aircraft Fuel System Diagram

The diagram illustrates the fuel system of an Airbus A220 aircraft. Key components and their connections are as follows:

- **Engines (ENG)**: Two engines are depicted, each connected to its respective fuel system.
- **Surge Tanks (L SURGE TANK, R SURGE TANK)**: Located on the left and right sides of the aircraft, these tanks manage fuel surges.
- **Main Tanks (L MAIN TANK, R MAIN TANK)**: These are the primary fuel storage tanks for each engine.
- **Center Tank**: A central fuel tank that connects to both main tanks.
- **Dry Bays**: Areas within the main tanks that are kept dry to prevent water accumulation.
- **Collector Tanks**: Positioned below the main tanks, these tanks collect fuel for distribution.
- **Scavenge Ejector Pumps**: Located at the bottom of the diagram, these pumps help in scavenging fuel from the collector tanks.
- **APU (Auxiliary Power Unit)**: Connected to the fuel system, providing power to the aircraft systems.

The diagram shows the flow of fuel from the surge tanks, through the main tanks, to the engines via the collector tanks. The scavenge ejector pumps ensure efficient fuel collection and distribution.
```

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Evaluation of impact on structural integrity and safety

nc_event_id: ATA-28-1d8db8329f3e8f0-6946
nc_event_date: 2022-11-17T05:52:01.011Z
task_date:2022-11-21T05:52:01.011Z
author:JEAN-MICHEL DUBOIS
role:Design Office

### Évaluation de l'impact
- Impact direct: potentiel fuite de carburant, risque incendie en vol
- Impact structurel: non directement affecté, mais risque de fuite critique
- Impact réglementaire: conformité, certification, nécessité de rapport
- Analyse préliminaire: pas de déformation ou dommage mécanique visible, mais risque de défaillance sous contrainte
- Recommandation: effectuer tests de pression et étanchéité approfondis
- Risques résiduels: si non traité, fuite en vol, incendie, perte de navigabilité
- Conclusion: impact critique, nécessite correction immédiate

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Impact assessment and mitigation options

nc_event_id: ATA-28-1d8db8329f3e07f4-15bc
nc_event_date: 2022-01-07T21:52:01.013Z
task_date:2022-01-11T10:00:00.000Z
author:Jean-Michel Tremblay
role:Design Office

Impact potentiel : fuite de carburant, contamination environnementale, risque incendie, rework coûteux (drainage, nettoyage). Impact quantitatif : coût sensor $1,800 par incident, durée d'arrêt ligne 3-5 heures. Si non traité : risque de fuite en service, feu, panne moteur. Mitigation proposée : recalibrer tous les outils de couple, effectuer tests d'étanchéité, passage en revue des procédures de montage, validation avant livraison. Vérifier la traçabilité des pièces et la conformité des composants. Plan d'action : programme de calibration renforcé, audits réguliers, formation continue, contrôle de lot de pièces, vérification de la procédure de montage.

##ATA 24 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Initial review of non-conformity report

nc_event_id: ATA-24-3889163cf136607e-ae89
nc_event_date: 2024-09-09T15:52:00.952Z
task_date:2024-09-09T15:52:00.952Z
author:JEAN_PIERRE LEBLANC
role:Design Office

Examination sommaire du rapport NC initial. La non-conformité concerne la valve de résistance du réservoir de carburant en position inversée. Cause probable liée à l'installation de la ligne de ventilation carburant, marquage d'orientation lors de l'assemblage. Impact potentiel : surpression/ sous-pression, dégradation de câbles, rework. Nécessité d'une analyse approfondie pour confirmer la cause, évaluer la criticité et déterminer la portée structurelle. Priorité à la compréhension des processus d'installation et de contrôle en ligne. Recommandation : planifier une inspection visuelle complémentaire, vérifier la traçabilité du batch, et analyser la procédure d'assemblage pour déceler erreurs récurrentes.

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Electrical System Impact and Diagnostics

nc_event_id: ATA-28-40a87131a4d53bd8-8abb
nc_event_date: 2023-10-31T11:52:01.002Z
task_date:2023-11-02T09:00:00.000Z
author:Marie-Claude Leblanc
role:Design Office Expert Elec

### Diagnostic approfondi
- Capteur Fuel Quantity (PN 67411-222) connecté au système fuel computer, interface électrique critique.
- Anomalies capacitance indiquent possible défaillance électrique ou dérive calibration.
- Vérifier la connectique, câblage, et alimentation électrique du capteur.
- Analyser logs système pour détecter autres anomalies ou erreurs associées.
- Vérifier si défaillance électrique pourrait affecter la fiabilité du système fuel, notamment en vol.

### Recommandations
- Effectuer tests électriques sur capteur et connectique.
- Vérifier la conformité électrique selon spécifications.
- Si défaillance électrique confirmée, planifier remplacement ou réparation.
- Mettre en place un contrôle renforcé pour lot de capteurs, incluant tests électriques et calibration.
- Documenter toutes les anomalies électriques, et rapporter au fournisseur.
- Synthèse : défaillance électrique probable, impact sur la précision des indications fuel, correction par remplacement ou réparation.
- Plan d'action : tests électriques, vérification de la connectique, remplacement si nécessaire, suivi qualité fournisseur.

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 000: Fuel tank resistance valve installed in inverted position

nc_event_id: ATA-24-4467583cf763993e-3a05
nc_event_date: 2024-03-23T16:52:00.958Z
task_date:2024-03-23T16:52:00.958Z
author:Jean-Pierre Leblanc

Report concerning the non-conformity observed regarding the examination of pressure filter elements. Specific observations include the presence of collected debris within the filter elements. Noted issues with part number FF-9101, serial 21219, supplied by FilterSupreme. Immediate concerns regarding potential impacts on fuel flow and overall system performance.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Analysis of Non-Conformity

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T14:30:00.000Z
author:Julien Tremblay
role:Design Office

### Analysis of Non-Conformity

The non-conformity regarding the blocked tank filter has been thoroughly examined. The primary cause identified is the contamination due to improper sealing techniques during the installation of fuel tank filters. The use of non-standard filters has contributed to this issue, leading to blockages that could halt performance and necessitate maintenance interventions.

### Causes Identified
- **Internal Causes:** Failure to adhere to specified installation procedures and use of non-standard parts.
- **External Causes:** Supplier quality issues related to the filters provided.

### Impact Assessment
- **Operational Impact:** Potential for in-flight shutdowns if not addressed.
- **Financial Impact:** Estimated maintenance costs could rise by approximately $20,000, affecting production and increasing fleet-wide operational costs.

### Proposed Action Plan
1. **Immediate Replacement:** Replace the affected filters with specified components.
2. **Supplier Review:** Conduct a review of the supplier's quality assurance processes.
3. **Training:** Implement additional training for technicians on installation standards.

### Conclusion
This analysis highlights critical areas for immediate attention to mitigate risks associated with the blocked tank filter. The proposed actions aim to enhance reliability and ensure compliance with safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Expert Review of Hydro System

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T15:00:00.000Z
author:Sophie Gagnon
role:Design Office Expert Hydro

The hydro system's performance must be evaluated in relation to the blocked tank filter issue. It is crucial to understand how the blockage affects the overall fuel flow and pressure within the system. Furthermore, the potential for debris accumulation in other components must be assessed to prevent cascading failures. A comprehensive review of the hydro system's design and operational parameters is necessary to ensure that all aspects are considered. This includes detailed flow analysis, pressure testing, and the evaluation of filter specifications to ensure compliance with industry standards. Recommendations for modifications or enhancements to the system should be documented.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Synthesis of Expert Review

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T16:30:00.000Z
author:Julien Tremblay
role:Design Office

### Synthesis of Expert Review

Following the expert review of the hydro system, it is confirmed that the blockage in the tank filter significantly impacts fuel flow and pressure, which could lead to further complications if not addressed. 

### Key Findings
- **Flow Analysis:** Blockages have been confirmed to reduce flow rates below acceptable thresholds.
- **Pressure Testing:** Initial tests indicate pressure drops that could affect system performance.

### Revised Action Plan
1. **Immediate System Flush:** Conduct a system flush to remove debris.
2. **Filter Specification Review:** Ensure all filters meet the required specifications.
3. **Ongoing Monitoring:** Implement a monitoring system for early detection of blockages.

### Conclusion
The findings necessitate immediate action to prevent further operational risks. The revised action plan aims to enhance system reliability and maintain compliance with safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Validation of Analysis and Action Plan

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-11T12:00:00.000Z
author:Éric Roy
role:Stress Office

### Structural Analysis of Blocked Tank Filter Issue

Following the validation of the action plan concerning the blocked tank filter, a detailed structural analysis has been conducted to assess the implications of the proposed modifications on the aircraft's integrity and performance.

### Analysis Overview
- **Objective:** Ensure that the replacement of non-standard filters and the associated maintenance actions do not compromise the aircraft's structural integrity.
- **Methodology:** Utilized advanced modeling techniques through MATLAB and Simulink to simulate the stress distribution across the affected components.

### Key Findings
- **Static Analysis:** The replacement filters, when installed according to the specified guidelines, maintain the structural integrity of the fuel system under normal operating conditions. However, attention must be paid to the installation process to prevent misalignment.
- **Dynamic Analysis:** Simulations indicate that the flow dynamics within the fuel system are significantly improved with the new filters, reducing the risk of pressure fluctuations that could lead to structural fatigue over time.

### Recommendations
1. **Installation Protocol:** Strict adherence to installation protocols must be enforced to avoid internal misalignments that could lead to stress concentrations.
2. **Material Specifications:** Ensure that all replacement filters meet the specified material and design standards to withstand operational stresses.
3. **Ongoing Monitoring:** Implement a continuous monitoring system to detect early signs of blockage or pressure anomalies in the fuel system.
4. **Training for Technicians:** Provide comprehensive training for technicians on the importance of proper installation techniques to mitigate risks associated with structural integrity.

### Conclusion
The analysis confirms that the proposed actions can be implemented without compromising the aircraft's structural integrity. Continuous monitoring and adherence to installation protocols are crucial for maintaining safety and performance standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 300: Request for Additional Information

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-11T13:30:00.000Z
author:Léa Tremblay
role:Quality Controller

### Request for Additional Information

To ensure the accuracy of the stress analysis conducted by the Stress Office, I require further details regarding the following:
1. **Specific Installation Procedures:** A detailed outline of the installation procedures for the new filters.
2. **Supplier Quality Assurance:** Information on the quality assurance processes implemented by FilterSupreme to prevent future contamination issues.
3. **Monitoring Systems:** Details on the proposed monitoring systems for early detection of blockages and pressure anomalies.

This information is critical to validate the effectiveness of the proposed action plan and ensure compliance with safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 300: Response to Additional Information Request

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4

### Response to Quality Controller's Request

In response to the request for additional information, the following details have been compiled:

1. **Specific Installation Procedures:** The installation procedures for the new filters will include:
   - Pre-installation inspection of the filter housing.
   - Use of specified sealing techniques to prevent contamination.
   - Verification of alignment before securing the filters.

2. **Supplier Quality Assurance:** FilterSupreme has implemented the following quality assurance measures:
   - Regular audits of manufacturing processes.
   - Testing of filter materials for compliance with industry standards.
   - Documentation of all quality control measures taken during production.

3. **Monitoring Systems:** The proposed monitoring systems will include:
   - Installation of pressure sensors at critical points in the fuel system.
   - Real-time data logging and alert systems for early detection of anomalies.
   - Scheduled maintenance checks to ensure system integrity.

These measures are designed to enhance the reliability of the fuel system and mitigate risks associated with the blocked tank filter issue.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 400: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation après calculs

- **Structural Integrity Maintained:** The replacement of non-standard filters, when installed according to specified procedures, does not adversely affect the structural integrity of the fuel system.

### Final Analysis and Recommendations

#### Context
The non-conformity identified as blocked tank filters in the A220 fleet is primarily due to contamination from improper sealing techniques during installation. This has been compounded by the use of non-standard filters, leading to potential operational disruptions and increased maintenance costs.

#### Causes
- **Internal Causes:**
  - Installation errors due to inadequate technician training.
  - Failure to adhere to specified installation procedures.
- **External Causes:**
  - Supplier quality issues with non-standard filters.
  - Inadequate quality assurance processes at FilterSupreme.

#### Impact
- **Operational:** Risk of in-flight shutdowns if unresolved.
- **Financial:** Maintenance costs could increase by approximately $20,000 per incident.

#### Action Plan
1. **Immediate Filter Replacement:**
   - Replace all non-standard filters with compliant parts.
   - Conduct a system flush to remove existing debris.
2. **Supplier Audit:**
   - Review and enhance supplier quality assurance processes.
3. **Technician Training:**
   - Implement comprehensive training on installation standards.
4. **Monitoring System:**
   - Install pressure sensors for early detection of blockages.
   - Schedule regular maintenance checks.

#### Conclusion
The proposed action plan is approved for immediate implementation. Continuous monitoring and adherence to installation protocols are essential to maintain safety and performance standards. Follow-up reviews will assess the effectiveness of these measures to ensure compliance with industry standards.

author:Dominic Lavoie
role:Design Office

# Analysis of Non-Conformity Report

## Overview
The non-conformity labeled 'Cracked line obstruction leading to flow load' has been thoroughly examined based on the initial report provided by the Quality Controler. The primary cause identified is the accumulation of debris in the fuel during the forging process, which has led to inadequate filtration maintenance. This analysis aims to delve deeper into the underlying causes, assess the impacts, and propose a comprehensive action plan.

## Examination of the Report
Upon reviewing the report, the following key points were noted:
- **Non-Destructive Testing Results**: The report indicates abnormally low X-ray rates, which raises immediate concerns regarding the integrity of the fuel system, particularly in part number FL091, serial 32310, supplied by Supplier B.
- **Impact on Engine Performance**: The direct implications of this non-conformity include engine efficiency performance issues and potential engine shutdowns, which could lead to significant operational disruptions.

## Identification of Causes
### Internal Causes
- **Filtration Material Performance**: The filtering material used in the fuel system has been identified as inadequate, contributing to the debris accumulation.
- **Maintenance Practices**: Overdue maintenance checks have been detected, indicating a lapse in scheduled operational maintenance.

### External Causes
- **Supplier Quality Issues**: The quality of the supplied components, particularly the fuel system parts, may not meet the required standards, leading to performance degradation.

## Evaluation of Impacts
- **Operational Readiness**: The potential cost implications of $20,000 per incident highlight the financial burden on operational readiness of the aircraft.
- **Risk of Catastrophic Failure**: If left unaddressed, prolonged neglect of this issue may result in catastrophic engine failure due to inflight shutdowns, posing safety risks.

## Proposed Action Plan
1. **Immediate Mitigation Measures**: Schedule operational maintenance for the filtration systems to restore normal operations and prevent further incidents.
2. **Supplier Engagement**: Initiate discussions with Supplier B to address quality concerns and ensure compliance with specifications.
3. **Enhanced Monitoring**: Implement a more rigorous monitoring system for fuel flow reduction incidents to detect issues earlier.
4. **Review of Maintenance Protocols**: Revise maintenance schedules and protocols to ensure timely checks and balances are in place.

## Conclusion
This analysis underscores the critical nature of the identified non-conformity and the necessity for immediate corrective actions. The proposed plan aims to mitigate risks effectively and ensure the continued safety and efficiency of the aircraft operations. The next step is to submit this analysis and action plan for validation by the Design Manager.\n\n##ATA 71 (Powerplant) - 200: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation Finale avant calculs

Utilizing advanced modeling software (MATLAB and Simulink), the following assessments were performed:
- **Static Structural Analysis**: Evaluated the impact of the proposed maintenance on the fuel system's structural integrity.
- **Dynamic Response Analysis**: Assessed potential effects during operational conditions, including engine performance and fuel flow dynamics.

## Findings
- **Internal Causes**: The inadequate filtration material and overdue maintenance practices were confirmed to pose risks to structural integrity.
- **External Causes**: Supplier quality issues were identified as critical factors affecting the performance of part number FL091.

## Recommendations
1. **Immediate Structural Reinforcement**: Implement structural reinforcements in the fuel system to accommodate the expected operational loads post-maintenance.
2. **Supplier Quality Assurance**: Engage with Supplier B to ensure that all components meet the required standards before installation.
3. **Monitoring System Implementation**: Establish a real-time monitoring system for fuel flow and structural integrity to detect anomalies early.
4. **Periodic Review of Structural Integrity**: Schedule regular assessments of the fuel system's structural performance to ensure ongoing compliance with safety standards.

## Conclusion
The structural analysis indicates that with the proposed actions, the integrity of the aircraft can be maintained. Immediate implementation of the recommendations is crucial to mitigate risks associated with the identified non-conformity. Further collaboration with the Quality Controller and Design Manager may be necessary to refine the action plan.\n\n##ATA 71 (Powerplant) - 400: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation après calculs

Examination sommaire du rapport NC initial. La non-conformité concerne la valve de résistance du réservoir de carburant en position inversée. Cause probable liée à l'installation de la ligne de ventilation carburant, marquage d'orientation lors de l'assemblage. Impact potentiel : surpression/ sous-pression, dégradation de câbles, rework. Nécessité d'une analyse approfondie pour confirmer la cause, évaluer la criticité et déterminer la portée structurelle. Priorité à la compréhension des processus d'installation et de contrôle en ligne. Recommandation : planifier une inspection visuelle complémentaire, vérifier la traçabilité du batch, et analyser la procédure d'assemblage pour déceler erreurs récurrentes.

### Analysis of Non-Conformity Report

- **Report Overview**: Received report regarding the blocked tank filter issue. Significant debris accumulation observed in filter elements.
- **Affected Component**: Part number FF-9101, Serial number 38542, Supplier: FilterSupreme.
- **Initial Findings**: Contamination in the fuel system is the primary cause, traced back to non-standard filters.

### Identification of Causes
- **Primary Cause**: Non-standard filters not meeting specifications.
- **Secondary Causes**: Potential supplier quality issues and lack of adherence to installation protocols.
- **Internal Causes**: Possible assembly errors or inadequate quality checks during installation.
- **External Causes**: Supplier defects leading to contamination.

### Impact Assessment
- **Impact on Navigability**: Potential engine performance issues could lead to maintenance interventions.
- **Quantitative Impact**: Estimated maintenance costs of $20,000 per aircraft and increased downtime by 2 days.
- **Risks if Unaddressed**: Increased risk of engine failure, leading to potential shutdowns and emergency situations.

### Proposed Mitigation Plan
- **Immediate Action**: Replace non-standard filters with standard specified filters to resolve flow issues.
- **Long-term Solution**: Implement stricter supplier quality controls and filter specifications.
- **Monitoring Metrics**: Track blockage incidents at a rate of 0.2 incidents per 100 installations.

### Conclusion

task_date:2023-03-20T12:30:00.000Z
author:Jean Dupont
role:Design Office

### Analysis of Non-Conformity

Upon reviewing the non-conformity report regarding the degraded pressure air performance tank, the following observations have been made:

1. **Nature of Non-Conformity**: The contamination detected during gas flow detection has been linked to foreign fatigue introduced by manual component storage conditions. This has resulted in suboptimal pressure regulation, specifically affecting the A220 specifications.

2. **Primary Causes**: The primary cause identified is material degradation due to supplier exposure. This necessitates adherence to stringent storage protocols and sensor calibration measures specific to the supplier.

3. **Impact Assessment**: The contamination may significantly hinder airflow pressure, affecting the cabin environmental control onboard. Quantitatively, losses could tally up to $50,000 per aircraft if multiple components require rectification. If left unresolved, this could lead to inconsistent system pressures, potentially resulting in safety risks during flight operations, including return-to-gate scenarios.

### Proposed Action Plan

To mitigate the identified issues, the following actions are recommended:

- **Immediate Assessment**: Conduct a thorough assessment of the affected components, particularly focusing on the part number P765-47 and serial number SN555912 from PressureTech Dynamics.
- **Supplier Collaboration**: Engage with the supplier to ensure compliance with storage protocols and to discuss potential upgrades in handling processes to minimize future risks.
- **Monitoring and Metrics**: Implement a monitoring system to track the frequency of incidences related to defects, aiming for a reduction in the current rate of 3 parts at a 1000% defect rate.
- **Preemptive Upgrades**: Explore options for preemptive upgrades in the storage and handling processes to significantly reduce the risk of contamination.

### Conclusion

This analysis highlights the critical need for immediate action to address the contamination issue and prevent further degradation of component performance. The proposed action plan aims to ensure compliance with industry standards and maintain the navigability and reliability of the A220 aircraft.\n\n##ATA 36 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Expert Review on Electrical Impact

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Electrical System Impact and Diagnostics

nc_event_id: ATA-28-40a87131a4d53bd8-8abb
nc_event_date: 2023-10-31T11:52:01.002Z
task_date:2023-11-02T09:00:00.000Z
author:Marie-Claude Leblanc
role:Design Office Expert Elec

### Diagnostic approfondi
- Capteur Fuel Quantity (PN 67411-222) connecté au système fuel computer, interface électrique critique.
- Anomalies capacitance indiquent possible défaillance électrique ou dérive calibration.
- Vérifier la connectique, câblage, et alimentation électrique du capteur.
- Analyser logs système pour détecter autres anomalies ou erreurs associées.
- Vérifier si défaillance électrique pourrait affecter la fiabilité du système fuel, notamment en vol.

### Recommandations
- Effectuer tests électriques sur capteur et connectique.
- Vérifier la conformité électrique selon spécifications.
- Si défaillance électrique confirmée, planifier remplacement ou réparation.
- Mettre en place un contrôle renforcé pour lot de capteurs, incluant tests électriques et calibration.
- Documenter toutes les anomalies électriques, et rapporter au fournisseur.
- Synthèse : défaillance électrique probable, impact sur la précision des indications fuel, correction par remplacement ou réparation.
- Plan d'action : tests électriques, vérification de la connectique, remplacement si nécessaire, suivi qualité fournisseur.

Immediate implications for actuator performance and system reliability noted. nc_event_id: ATA-29-34309a46aa0d7333-0443

task_date:2022-04-26T07:40:27.213Z
author:Dominic Lavoie
role:Design Office

### Analysis of Non-Conformity

The non-conformity labeled 'Contaminated hydraulic fluid' has been reviewed. The primary cause identified is contamination from particulates during actuator transfer from poorly maintained reservoirs. This indicates inadequate maintenance procedures and equipment degradation.

### Impact Assessment

- **Accelerated actuator wear** affecting components.
- **Surface system reliability** is compromised.
- **Quantitative Impact**: Mitigation costs estimated at $15,000 per incident, with a reduction in component lifespan by an undetermined percentage.

### Recommendations

1. **Enhanced Filtration**: Implement improved filtration systems to prevent particulate contamination.
2. **Periodic Checks**: Establish routine inspections of hydraulic fluid to ensure cleanliness.
3. **Maintenance Protocols**: Revise maintenance procedures to ensure reservoirs are properly maintained and monitored.

### Next Steps

- Collaborate with Quality Control for further investigation into contamination sources.
- Prepare for potential consultation with Design Office Experts in hydraulics to refine mitigation strategies.\n\n##ATA 29 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Expert Review of Mitigation Strategies

nc_event_id: ATA-29-34309a46aa0d7333-0443
nc_event_date: 2022-04-26T05:40:27.213Z
task_date:2022-04-27T09:15:27.213Z
author:Jean Dupuis
role:Design Office Expert Hydro

The initial analysis provided by Dominic Lavoie has been reviewed. The contamination issue is critical and requires a thorough approach. 

### Detailed Recommendations

- **Filtration Systems**: It is crucial to select filtration systems that can handle the specific particulate sizes identified in the fluid samples. Consideration should be given to the flow rates and pressure drops across the filters.
- **Inspection Protocols**: Develop a comprehensive inspection protocol that includes not only visual inspections but also fluid sampling at regular intervals. This will help in early detection of contamination.
- **Training**: Ensure that maintenance personnel are trained on the importance of maintaining hydraulic reservoirs and the potential risks associated with neglect.

### Conclusion

The recommendations provided are essential for mitigating the risks associated with contaminated hydraulic fluid. However, further collaboration with the Design Office is necessary to finalize the action plan.\n\n##ATA 29 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Synthesis of Analysis and Recommendations

nc_event_id: ATA-29-34309a46aa0d7333-0443
nc_event_date: 2022-04-26T05:40:27.213Z
task_date:2022-04-28T10:30:27.213Z
author:Dominic Lavoie
role:Design Office

### Final Analysis and Recommendations

Following the expert review by Jean Dupuis, the following consolidated recommendations are proposed:

1. **Implement Enhanced Filtration**: Select and install filtration systems capable of removing identified particulate sizes.
2. **Establish Routine Fluid Inspections**: Create a schedule for fluid sampling and analysis to monitor contamination levels.
3. **Revise Maintenance Procedures**: Update maintenance protocols to include regular checks and cleaning of hydraulic reservoirs.
4. **Personnel Training**: Conduct training sessions for maintenance staff on the importance of hydraulic fluid integrity.

### Submission for Validation

### Structural Stress Analysis for Contaminated Hydraulic Fluid Issue

Following the validation of the action plan regarding the non-conformity of 'Contaminated hydraulic fluid', a detailed structural stress analysis has been conducted to assess the implications of the proposed mitigation strategies on the aircraft structure, particularly focusing on the actuator systems.

### Analysis Overview

1. **Static Structural Analysis**: The static loads on the actuator systems were modeled using MATLAB to evaluate the stress distribution under normal operating conditions. The results indicate that the proposed enhanced filtration system will not adversely affect the load-bearing capacity of the actuator mounts.
   - **Findings**: No significant increase in stress levels was observed, ensuring that the structural integrity remains intact.

2. **Dynamic Structural Analysis**: A dynamic analysis was performed to assess the impact of potential vibrations introduced by the new filtration systems. Simulations in Simulink revealed that the additional components would introduce minimal dynamic loads, which are within acceptable limits for the A220 design specifications.
   - **Findings**: The dynamic response of the actuator systems remains compliant with the required performance standards.

### Recommendations

- **Implementation of Enhanced Filtration**: Proceed with the installation of the recommended filtration systems, ensuring they are compatible with existing actuator designs.
- **Monitoring System**: Develop a monitoring system to continuously assess the performance of the filtration units and their impact on hydraulic fluid quality.
- **Periodic Structural Reviews**: Schedule periodic reviews of the actuator systems post-implementation to ensure no unforeseen issues arise from the new components.

### Conclusion

The structural integrity of the A220 is maintained with the proposed changes. Immediate action on the recommendations is advised to prevent further contamination incidents and ensure operational efficiency. Should further information or clarification be required, please do not hesitate to reach out.\n\n##ATA 29 (Aircraft Systems) - 300: Request for Additional Information

nc_event_date: 2023-03-20T10:40:28.123Z
task_date:2023-03-20T12:30:00.000Z
author:Jean Dupont
role:Design Office

### Analysis of Non-Conformity

Upon reviewing the non-conformity report regarding the degraded pressure air performance tank, the following observations have been made:

1. **Nature of Non-Conformity**: The contamination detected during gas flow detection has been linked to foreign fatigue introduced by manual component storage conditions. This has resulted in suboptimal pressure regulation, specifically affecting the A220 specifications.

2. **Primary Causes**: The primary cause identified is material degradation due to supplier exposure. This necessitates adherence to stringent storage protocols and sensor calibration measures specific to the supplier.

3. **Impact Assessment**: The contamination may significantly hinder airflow pressure, affecting the cabin environmental control onboard. Quantitatively, losses could tally up to $50,000 per aircraft if multiple components require rectification. If left unresolved, this could lead to inconsistent system pressures, potentially resulting in safety risks during flight operations, including return-to-gate scenarios.

### Proposed Action Plan

To mitigate the identified issues, the following actions are recommended:

- **Immediate Assessment**: Conduct a thorough assessment of the affected components, particularly focusing on the part number P765-47 and serial number SN555912 from PressureTech Dynamics.
- **Supplier Collaboration**: Engage with the supplier to ensure compliance with storage protocols and to discuss potential upgrades in handling processes to minimize future risks.
- **Monitoring and Metrics**: Implement a monitoring system to track the frequency of incidences related to defects, aiming for a reduction in the current rate of 3 parts at a 1000% defect rate.
- **Preemptive Upgrades**: Explore options for preemptive upgrades in the storage and handling processes to significantly reduce the risk of contamination.

### Conclusion

This analysis highlights the critical need for immediate action to address the contamination issue and prevent further degradation of component performance. The proposed action plan aims to ensure compliance with industry standards and maintain the navigability and reliability of the A220 aircraft.\n\n##ATA 36 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Expert Review on Electrical Impact

The analysis confirms that the contamination could lead to electrical failures in the pressure regulation sensors. Recommend immediate recalibration of sensors and implementation of enhanced protective measures against contamination.\n\n##ATA 36 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Final Analysis and Submission for Validation

1. **Nature of Non-Conformity**: Contamination detected during gas flow detection.
2. **Primary Causes**: Material degradation due to inadequate storage conditions and supplier exposure.
3. **Impact Assessment**: Potential financial losses and safety risks if unresolved.
4. **Action Plan**:
   - Immediate assessment of affected components.
   - Collaboration with the supplier for compliance and upgrades.
   - Monitoring system implementation.

5. **Electrical Considerations**: Immediate recalibration of sensors and enhanced protective measures against contamination.

### Conclusion

- **Operational**: Immediate corrective actions are required to prevent recurrence and ensure compliance.

#### Proposed Action Plan
1. **Immediate Replacement**: Faulty sensors and wiring must be replaced without delay to restore operational integrity.
2. **Enhanced Testing Protocols**: Implement rigorous calibration verification processes to prevent future occurrences.
3. **Supplier Review**: Conduct a thorough review of SensorTech's quality assurance processes to ensure component reliability.
4. **Monitoring and Compliance**: Post-repair monitoring of stress levels in the fuel pump area to ensure ongoing compliance.

#### Conclusion
The outlined actions are critical to maintaining safety and reliability in aircraft operations. Immediate execution is necessary to mitigate risks and ensure compliance with operational standards. The approach prioritizes scenarios with direct implications on navigability, resistance, and production costs, ensuring a realistic and industry-contextualized solution.

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 000: Debris Collection in Filter Elements

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
author:Léa Tremblay
role:Quality Controler

Report concerning the non-conformity observed regarding the examination of pressure filter elements. Specific observations include the presence of collected debris within the filter elements. Noted issues with part number FF-9101, serial 21219, supplied by FilterSupreme. Immediate concerns regarding potential impacts on fuel flow and overall system performance.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Analysis of Non-Conformity

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T14:30:00.000Z
author:Julien Tremblay
role:Design Office

### Analysis of Non-Conformity

The non-conformity regarding the blocked tank filter has been thoroughly examined. The primary cause identified is the contamination due to improper sealing techniques during the installation of fuel tank filters. The use of non-standard filters has contributed to this issue, leading to blockages that could halt performance and necessitate maintenance interventions.

### Causes Identified
- **Internal Causes:** Failure to adhere to specified installation procedures and use of non-standard parts.
- **External Causes:** Supplier quality issues related to the filters provided.

### Impact Assessment
- **Operational Impact:** Potential for in-flight shutdowns if not addressed.
- **Financial Impact:** Estimated maintenance costs could rise by approximately $20,000, affecting production and increasing fleet-wide operational costs.

### Proposed Action Plan
1. **Immediate Replacement:** Replace the affected filters with specified components.
2. **Supplier Review:** Conduct a review of the supplier's quality assurance processes.
3. **Training:** Implement additional training for technicians on installation standards.

### Conclusion
This analysis highlights critical areas for immediate attention to mitigate risks associated with the blocked tank filter. The proposed actions aim to enhance reliability and ensure compliance with safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Expert Review of Hydro System

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T15:00:00.000Z
author:Sophie Gagnon
role:Design Office Expert Hydro

The hydro system's performance must be evaluated in relation to the blocked tank filter issue. It is crucial to understand how the blockage affects the overall fuel flow and pressure within the system. Furthermore, the potential for debris accumulation in other components must be assessed to prevent cascading failures. A comprehensive review of the hydro system's design and operational parameters is necessary to ensure that all aspects are considered. This includes detailed flow analysis, pressure testing, and the evaluation of filter specifications to ensure compliance with industry standards. Recommendations for modifications or enhancements to the system should be documented.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Synthesis of Expert Review

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T16:30:00.000Z
author:Julien Tremblay
role:Design Office

### Synthesis of Expert Review

Following the expert review of the hydro system, it is confirmed that the blockage in the tank filter significantly impacts fuel flow and pressure, which could lead to further complications if not addressed. 

### Key Findings
- **Flow Analysis:** Blockages have been confirmed to reduce flow rates below acceptable thresholds.
- **Pressure Testing:** Initial tests indicate pressure drops that could affect system performance.

### Revised Action Plan
1. **Immediate System Flush:** Conduct a system flush to remove debris.
2. **Filter Specification Review:** Ensure all filters meet the required specifications.
3. **Ongoing Monitoring:** Implement a monitoring system for early detection of blockages.

### Conclusion
The findings necessitate immediate action to prevent further operational risks. The revised action plan aims to enhance system reliability and maintain compliance with safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Validation of Analysis and Action Plan

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-10T17:00:00.000Z
author:Marc Dupuis
role:Design Manager

### Validation of Analysis and Action Plan

The analysis conducted by the Design Office and the expert review have been thoroughly evaluated. The proposed actions are deemed appropriate and necessary to mitigate the risks associated with the blocked tank filter. 

### Approval
- **Action Plan:** Approved for immediate implementation.
- **Monitoring:** Continuous monitoring and reporting will be required to ensure effectiveness.

### Next Steps
- Initiate the action plan and schedule follow-up reviews to assess the effectiveness of the implemented measures.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 200: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation Finale avant calculs

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-11T10:30:00.000Z
author:Philippe Gagnon
role:Design Manager

### Validation of Analysis and Action Plan

Analysis reviewed. Action plan valid. Immediate actions necessary for blocked tank filter issue.

### Causes Identified
- **Internal Causes:** Installation errors, improper sealing techniques.
- **External Causes:** Supplier defects, non-standard filter usage.

### Recommendations
1. **Filter Replacement:** Replace non-standard filters with compliant parts.
2. **Supplier Audit:** Review supplier quality processes.
3. **Technician Training:** Enhance training on installation standards.
4. **System Flush:** Perform flush to remove debris.
5. **Monitoring System:** Implement for early blockage detection.

### Conclusion
Actions approved for implementation. Follow-up reviews required to assess effectiveness.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 300: Structural Analysis and Recommendations

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-11T12:00:00.000Z
author:Éric Roy
role:Stress Office

### Structural Analysis of Blocked Tank Filter Issue

Following the validation of the action plan concerning the blocked tank filter, a detailed structural analysis has been conducted to assess the implications of the proposed modifications on the aircraft's integrity and performance.

### Analysis Overview
- **Objective:** Ensure that the replacement of non-standard filters and the associated maintenance actions do not compromise the aircraft's structural integrity.
- **Methodology:** Utilized advanced modeling techniques through MATLAB and Simulink to simulate the stress distribution across the affected components.

### Key Findings
- **Static Analysis:** The replacement filters, when installed according to the specified guidelines, maintain the structural integrity of the fuel system under normal operating conditions. However, attention must be paid to the installation process to prevent misalignment.
- **Dynamic Analysis:** Simulations indicate that the flow dynamics within the fuel system are significantly improved with the new filters, reducing the risk of pressure fluctuations that could lead to structural fatigue over time.

### Recommendations
1. **Installation Protocol:** Strict adherence to installation protocols must be enforced to avoid internal misalignments that could lead to stress concentrations.
2. **Material Specifications:** Ensure that all replacement filters meet the specified material and design standards to withstand operational stresses.
3. **Ongoing Monitoring:** Implement a continuous monitoring system to detect early signs of blockage or pressure anomalies in the fuel system.
4. **Training for Technicians:** Provide comprehensive training for technicians on the importance of proper installation techniques to mitigate risks associated with structural integrity.

### Conclusion
The analysis confirms that the proposed actions can be implemented without compromising the aircraft's structural integrity. Continuous monitoring and adherence to installation protocols are crucial for maintaining safety and performance standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 300: Request for Additional Information

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-11T13:30:00.000Z
author:Léa Tremblay
role:Quality Controller

### Request for Additional Information

To ensure the accuracy of the stress analysis conducted by the Stress Office, I require further details regarding the following:
1. **Specific Installation Procedures:** A detailed outline of the installation procedures for the new filters.
2. **Supplier Quality Assurance:** Information on the quality assurance processes implemented by FilterSupreme to prevent future contamination issues.
3. **Monitoring Systems:** Details on the proposed monitoring systems for early detection of blockages and pressure anomalies.

This information is critical to validate the effectiveness of the proposed action plan and ensure compliance with safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 300: Response to Additional Information Request

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-11T14:00:00.000Z
author:Éric Roy
role:Stress Office

### Response to Quality Controller's Request

In response to the request for additional information, the following details have been compiled:

1. **Specific Installation Procedures:** The installation procedures for the new filters will include:
   - Pre-installation inspection of the filter housing.
   - Use of specified sealing techniques to prevent contamination.
   - Verification of alignment before securing the filters.

2. **Supplier Quality Assurance:** FilterSupreme has implemented the following quality assurance measures:
   - Regular audits of manufacturing processes.
   - Testing of filter materials for compliance with industry standards.
   - Documentation of all quality control measures taken during production.

3. **Monitoring Systems:** The proposed monitoring systems will include:
   - Installation of pressure sensors at critical points in the fuel system.
   - Real-time data logging and alert systems for early detection of anomalies.
   - Scheduled maintenance checks to ensure system integrity.

These measures are designed to enhance the reliability of the fuel system and mitigate risks associated with the blocked tank filter issue.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 400: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation après calculs

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-12T15:30:00.000Z
author:Gabriel Bouchard
role:Stress Manager

### Validation of Stress Analysis and Action Plan

Following the completion of the stress analysis regarding the blocked tank filter issue, I have reviewed the calculations and their implications on the aircraft's structural integrity. The analysis confirms that the proposed actions can be implemented without compromising safety standards.

### Key Findings
- **Structural Integrity Maintained:** The replacement of non-standard filters, when installed according to specified procedures, does not adversely affect the structural integrity of the fuel system.
- **Flow Dynamics Improved:** The new filters enhance flow dynamics, reducing the risk of pressure fluctuations that could lead to structural fatigue.

### Recommendations
1. **Strict Adherence to Installation Protocols:** Ensure that all technicians follow the detailed installation procedures to prevent misalignment and stress concentrations.
2. **Material Compliance:** Verify that all replacement filters meet the specified material and design standards.
3. **Continuous Monitoring:** Implement a robust monitoring system to detect early signs of blockage or pressure anomalies in the fuel system.
4. **Technician Training:** Provide comprehensive training on proper installation techniques to mitigate risks associated with structural integrity.

### Conclusion
The action plan is approved for implementation. Continuous monitoring and adherence to protocols are essential for maintaining performance and safety standards.\n\n##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 500: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation Finale

nc_event_id: ATA-28-79c879b5880cb1-53a4
nc_event_date: 2023-10-10T12:40:26.809Z
task_date:2023-10-14T10:45:00.000Z
author:Mathieu Gauthier
role:Quality Manager

### Final Analysis and Recommendations

#### Context
The non-conformity identified as blocked tank filters in the A220 fleet is primarily due to contamination from improper sealing techniques during installation. This has been compounded by the use of non-standard filters, leading to potential operational disruptions and increased maintenance costs.

#### Causes
- **Internal Causes:**
  - Installation errors due to inadequate technician training.
  - Failure to adhere to specified installation procedures.
- **External Causes:**
  - Supplier quality issues with non-standard filters.
  - Inadequate quality assurance processes at FilterSupreme.

#### Impact
- **Operational:** Risk of in-flight shutdowns if unresolved.
- **Financial:** Maintenance costs could increase by approximately $20,000 per incident.

#### Action Plan
1. **Immediate Filter Replacement:**
   - Replace all non-standard filters with compliant parts.
   - Conduct a system flush to remove existing debris.
2. **Supplier Audit:**
   - Review and enhance supplier quality assurance processes.
3. **Technician Training:**
   - Implement comprehensive training on installation standards.
4. **Monitoring System:**
   - Install pressure sensors for early detection of blockages.
   - Schedule regular maintenance checks.

#### Conclusion
The proposed action plan is approved for immediate implementation. Continuous monitoring and adherence to installation protocols are essential to maintain safety and performance standards. Follow-up reviews will assess the effectiveness of these measures to ensure compliance with industry standards.

##ATA 28 (Aircraft Systems) - 100: Evaluation of impact on structural integrity and safety

nc_event_id: ATA-28-1d8db8329f3e8f0-6946
nc_event_date: 2022-11-17T05:52:01.011Z
task_date:2022-11-21T05:52:01.011Z
author:JEAN-MICHEL DUBOIS
role:Design Office

### Évaluation de l'impact
- Impact direct: potentiel fuite de carburant, risque incendie en vol
- Impact structurel: non directement affecté, mais risque de fuite critique
- Impact réglementaire: conformité, certification, nécessité de rapport
- Analyse préliminaire: pas de déformation ou dommage mécanique visible, mais risque de défaillance sous contrainte
- Recommandation: effectuer tests de pression et étanchéité approfondis
- Risques résiduels: si non traité, fuite en vol, incendie, perte de navigabilité
- Conclusion: impact critique, nécessite correction immédiate

##ATA 55 (Structures) - 000: Material Thickness Loss Detected

nc_event_id: ATA-55-de7f337c0b48625b-55b3
nc_event_date: 2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
task_date:2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
author:Léa Tremblay
role:Quality Controler

Nondestructive testing performed on wing skin revealed a significant loss of material thickness. 
Specific areas of concern include the aluminum wing skin associated with part number WS-880-B, serial SN-2098 from supplier Allied Aviation Materials. 
No immediate structural issues noted, but further investigation required to assess long-term implications.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 100: Initial Analysis of Non-Conformity

nc_event_id: ATA-55-de7f337c0b48625b-55b3
nc_event_date: 2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
task_date:2022-08-18T15:00:00.000Z
author:Alexandre Tremblay
role:Design Office

### Analysis of Non-Conformity

- **Non-Conformity Label**: Corrosion on aluminum wing skin
- **Primary Cause**: Imperfect sealing allowing moisture ingress during flight operations, leading to long-term corrosion.
- **Analysis**: Corrosion results from inadequate sealing due to improper material selection, exacerbated by exposure to humid conditions.
- **Impact**: Potential for structural failure if left unaddressed, increased maintenance frequency.
- **Quantitative Impact**: Repair costs average $15,000 with potential downtime of 3 days per aircraft.
- **If Left Untreated**: Unchecked corrosion could lead to significant structural compromise requiring costly full wing panel replacement.
- **Mitigation Possible**: Post-correction sees the restoration of full corrosion resistance and material integrity.
- **Metrics**: Incident occurrence in 10% of fleet, with an average detection lag of 6 months.
- **ATA Domain**: ATA 55 Structures
- **Related ATA Domains**: ATA 55
- **Affected Parts**: Part number WS-880-B, serial SN-2098, supplier Allied Aviation Materials.
- **Observation Method**: Nondestructive testing indicating loss of material thickness.

### Recommendations
- Immediate inspection of affected components to assess corrosion extent.
- Review sealing materials and processes to prevent moisture ingress.
- Implement a monitoring program for early detection of similar issues in the fleet.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 100: Expert Review of Corrosion Analysis

nc_event_id: ATA-55-de7f337c0b48625b-55b3
nc_event_date: 2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
task_date:2022-08-19T09:30:00.000Z
author:Sophie Gagnon
role:Design Office Expert Structures

The analysis of corrosion on the aluminum wing skin has been reviewed. It is crucial to note that the sealing materials used in the assembly process may not be suitable for the environmental conditions encountered during flight operations. The moisture ingress has been confirmed as a significant factor contributing to the corrosion observed. 

### Additional Recommendations
- A comprehensive review of the sealing materials and their specifications is necessary to ensure compliance with environmental resistance standards. 
- Consideration should be given to the implementation of a more robust quality control process during assembly to prevent future occurrences. 
- It is also advisable to conduct a fleet-wide inspection of similar components to assess the potential for widespread issues.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 100: Consolidation of Analysis and Recommendations

nc_event_id: ATA-55-de7f337c0b48625b-55b3
nc_event_date: 2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
task_date:2022-08-19T15:00:00.000Z
author:Alexandre Tremblay
role:Design Office

### Consolidated Analysis

- **Non-Conformity**: Corrosion on aluminum wing skin due to inadequate sealing.
- **Primary Cause**: Moisture ingress from improper sealing materials.
- **Impact**: Risk of structural failure and increased maintenance costs.

### Action Plan
1. **Immediate Inspection**: Conduct thorough inspections of affected components.
2. **Material Review**: Assess and upgrade sealing materials to enhance moisture resistance.
3. **Quality Control**: Implement stricter quality control measures in the assembly process.
4. **Fleet Monitoring**: Establish a monitoring program for early detection of similar issues.

### Next Steps
- Submit this analysis and action plan for validation by the Design Manager.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 200: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation Finale avant calculs

nc_event_id: ATA-55-de7f337c0b48625b-55b3
nc_event_date: 2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
task_date:2022-08-20T10:15:00.000Z
author:Marc Gagnon
role:Design Manager

### Analysis Summary

- **Non-Conformity**: Corrosion on aluminum wing skin.
- **Causes**: 
  - **Internal**: Inadequate sealing during assembly.
  - **External**: Humid conditions during flight operations.

### Action Plan
1. **Immediate Inspection**: Inspect affected parts for corrosion extent.
2. **Material Review**: Evaluate sealing materials for humidity resistance.
3. **Quality Control**: Strengthen assembly quality checks.
4. **Fleet Monitoring**: Implement monitoring for early detection.

### Recommendation
Proceed with action plan. Ensure all steps are documented and monitored for effectiveness. No assumptions on structural impacts at this stage.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 300: Structural Analysis of Corrosion Impact

nc_event_id: ATA-55-de7f337c0b48625b-55b3
nc_event_date: 2022-08-18T14:40:28.658Z
task_date:2022-08-20T12:30:00.000Z
author:Éric Roy
role:Stress Office

### Structural Analysis Overview

Upon receiving the action plan regarding the corrosion issue on the aluminum wing skin, a detailed structural analysis was conducted to evaluate the implications of the identified corrosion on the aircraft's structural integrity.

### Analysis Details
- **Modeling Software Used**: MATLAB was employed to simulate the structural response of the wing under various load conditions, including static and dynamic scenarios.
- **Static Analysis**: The static analysis focused on the load-bearing capacity of the wing structure, considering the loss of material thickness due to corrosion. The results indicated that while the current structure may still meet minimum safety factors, the long-term implications of corrosion could lead to a gradual decline in structural integrity.
- **Dynamic Analysis**: A dynamic analysis was also performed to assess the wing's response to aerodynamic loads during flight. The findings suggested that the corrosion could adversely affect the wing's flutter characteristics, potentially leading to resonance issues under specific flight conditions.

### Recommendations
1. **Immediate Remediation**: It is critical to address the corrosion through repair or replacement of affected components to restore full structural integrity.
2. **Monitoring System**: Implement a continuous monitoring system to detect any further material loss or structural anomalies in real-time.
3. **Material Upgrade**: Consider upgrading to corrosion-resistant materials for future assemblies to mitigate similar issues.
4. **Re-evaluation of Design**: A comprehensive review of the design and assembly processes should be conducted to identify potential improvements in sealing techniques and material selection.

### Additional Information Required
To finalize the recommendations, further details regarding the operational environment and historical maintenance records of the affected aircraft are required. This information will help in assessing the risk of recurrence and in tailoring the corrective actions accordingly.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 300: Request for Additional Information

role:Quality Controller

To assist the Stress Office in finalizing their analysis and recommendations, please provide the following information:
- **Operational Environment**: Details regarding the typical flight conditions and environments where the affected aircraft operate.
- **Maintenance Records**: Historical maintenance logs for part number WS-880-B, serial SN-2098, including any previous inspections or repairs related to corrosion.

This data is crucial for understanding the context of the corrosion issue and for developing effective long-term solutions.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 300: Follow-up on Additional Information Request

Following the request for additional information regarding the operational environment and maintenance records, I would like to emphasize the importance of this data in refining our structural analysis. The insights gained from this information will not only aid in confirming the current findings but also in enhancing our recommendations for future preventive measures.

### Next Steps
- Await the provision of the requested data from the Quality Controller.
- Upon receipt, a reassessment of the structural analysis will be conducted to ensure all recommendations are comprehensive and contextually relevant.\n\n##ATA 55 (Structures) - 400: Validation de l'Analyse et Recommandation après calculs
